title: Star Wars: The Arcade Game

{{Quote|Now coin video game players can plunge head-on into the world of LUKE SKYWALKER in the ultimate fantasy...STAR WARS!|Flyer advertising the arcade game|<ref name="Flyer">{{WebCite|url=https://archive.org/details/arcadeflyer_star-wars|text=Atari: Star Wars flyer|date=1983}}</ref>}}
'''''Star Wars: The Arcade Game''''', or simply '''''Star Wars''''', is an [[Wikipedia:arcade game|arcade game]] produced by [[Atari]] and released on [[May 5]], [[1983]].<ref name="Copyright" /> The game is a first-person [[Wikipedia:space simulator|space simulator]], based around [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope'']]. More specifically the game's action covers the [[Battle of Yavin/Legends|Battle of Yavin]]. The game is composed of [[Wikipedia:3D computer graphics|3D]] color [[Wikipedia:vector graphics|vector graphics]]. Considered one of the most popular video games of all time, it was greatly successful in its day, particularly in the wake of the theatrical release of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']]. Several home versions have been released over the years.

==Opening crawl==
{{OpeningCrawl|
|name=Star Wars
|text=OBI-WAN KENOBI IS GONE BUT HIS
PRESENCE IS FELT WITHIN THE FORCE.
THE EMPIRE'S DEATH STAR, UNDER THE
COMMAND OF DARTH VADER, NEARS THE
REBEL PLANET. YOU MUST JOIN THE
REBELLION TO STOP THE EMPIRE.
THE FORCE WILL BE WITH YOU
ALWAYS}}

==Gameplay==
The player assumes the role of [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] ("Red Five"), as he pilots an [[T-65 X-wing starfighter/Legends|X-wing fighter]] from a first-person perspective. Unlike other arcade games of similar nature, the player does not have to destroy every enemy in order to advance through the game; he must simply survive as his fighter flies through the level, which most often means he must avoid or destroy the shots that enemies fire.

The player's ultimate goal is to destroy the [[DS-1 Orbital Battle Station/Legends|Death Star]] through three attack phases:

*'''Phase 1:''' The player begins in space above the Death Star. They must engage in a dog fight with [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] and enemy [[TIE/ln space superiority starfighter/Legends|TIE Fighters]].

*'''Phase 2:''' The player reaches the Death Star's surface as laser turrets on towers rise to confront the player. If the player manages to destroy all of the towers, they will receive a sizable point bonus.

*'''Phase 3:''' The player flies through the final trench towards the thermal exhaust port, the Death Star's weakness. Along the way they must avoid laser shots and various catwalks that block the path. At the end of the trench, the player must fly low in order to fire a [[Proton torpedo/Legends|proton torpedo]] into the port. If successful, the Death Star explodes and the game immediately restarts at a higher difficulty level. If unsuccessful, the player must restart the third phase from the beginning.

Each successive Death Star run greatly increases the difficulty; TIE Fighters shoot more often, there are more Laser towers and batteries in the second round, and there are many more obstacles and laser fire during the trench run. Unlike the movie, where the units shoot [[Laser cannon/Legends|laser cannons]], the enemy units in this game shoot projectiles resembling fireballs, in order to give the player a chance to destroy the shots fired at him.

Players with attention to detail may notice that after the TIE Fighter waves, when you are flying towards the Death Star, the yellow grid lines on the Death Star spell out either "MAY THE FORCE BE WITH YOU" on odd-numbered waves. Assorted peoples' names are spelled out on even numbered waves. When flying in the trench towards the vulnerable exhaust port, "USE THE FORCE" is spelled in green until you fire a shot. If you dodge the fireballs, do not fire any shots and use one shot to fire your torpedoes in to the exhaust port, you get a sizable "USE THE FORCE" bonus. This is encouraged by Obi Wan himself at the beginning of the trench sequence.

==Release and reception==
===Arcade version===
The game featured several digitized samples of voices from the movie, including [[Mark Hamill]] as Luke Skywalker, [[Alec Guinness]] as [[Obi-Wan Kenobi/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi]], [[James Earl Jones]] as Darth Vader, [[Harrison Ford]] as [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], and the mechanized beeps of [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]]. The game was the first to feature digitized voice capabilities, often considered a prime factor of the game's success. In some gameplay, the first level utilized [[The Imperial March]]. This was because the Arcade Game was actually released after the release of [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']].

The game is available as a standard upright or an elaborately decorated sit-down version. The controls consist of a yoke control (similar to a steering wheel) with several buttons, all which fired the fighter's laser.

The game could be converted into [[The Empire Strikes Back (arcade game)|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] arcade game.

===Home versions===
It was later produced as ''Star Wars: The Arcade Game'' for the [[Wikipedia:Atari 2600|Atari 2600]], [[Wikipedia:Atari 5200|Atari 5200]], [[Wikipedia:Atari 8-bit|Atari 8-bit home computers]], [[Wikipedia:ColecoVision|ColecoVision]], and [[Wikipedia:Commodore 64|Commodore 64]]. The [[Wikipedia:Commodore 64|Commodore 64]] version was programmed by [[Daniel James Gallagher]] and featured voice sampling by [[Andy Beverage]].

[[File:RS3-SWAG-BTS.jpg|thumb|left|250px|[[Star Wars: Rogue Squadron III: Rebel Strike|''Rebel Strike'']] director [[Julian Eggebrecht]] with the ''Star Wars'' cockpit arcade model]]
The game was published for home computers in Europe by [[Domark]] in [[1987]]. That same year [[Broderbund]] acquired the rights to develop ''Star Wars'' games from Lucasfilm. The [[Wikipedia:Mac (computer)|Mac]]intosh and [[Wikipedia:Amiga|Amiga]] versions of the arcade game were ported by Juergen Friedrich and published in North America by Broderbund in [[1989]]. In [[1991]], an updated version of the game was released for the NEC PC-98 and the Sharp X68000 systems under the title ''[[Star Wars: Attack on the Death Star]]''. This version featured a long introductory cutscene at the beginning of the game.

In [[2003]]'s [[Star Wars: Rogue Squadron III: Rebel Strike]], all 3 classic trilogy arcade games are unlockable (although ''Return of the Jedi'' requires a cheat to unlock). The appearance of the arcade system in ''Rebel Strike'' indicated that the version used was the upright version of the arcade cabinet, and not the sit down model.

==Legacy==
[[File:SW_Arcade-Galactic_Spy.jpg|thumb|right|250px|The game cabinet in ''Star Wars Galactic Spy'']]
Concepts original to the game, most notably the [[Meridian trench/Legends|Meridian trench]] being loaded with obstacles instead of barren as in the original film, were later reused for the Battle of Yavin mission in the 2001 game ''Star Wars: Rogue Squadron II: Rogue Leader'', as well as its co-op port in ''Star Wars: Rogue Squadron III: Rebel Strike''. The latter game also included the arcade game as an unlockable bonus. According to the making of [[The Making of Star Wars: Rogue Squadron III: Rebel Strike|documentary]] for ''Rebel Strike'', the arcade game acted as the inspiration of the ''Rogue Squadron'' series.

The [[2012]] non-canon web-based video game ''[[Star Wars Galactic Spy]]'' included the arcade cabinet as one of the items players can search for in [[Mos Eisley/Legends|Mos Eisley]]. [[Stormtrooper/Legends|Stormtroopers]] are seen waiting for their turn to play the game.

In the films [[Wikipedia:Gremlins|''Gremlins'']] and [[Wikipedia:No Small Affair|''No Small Affair'']] (both released [[1984]]), both [[Wikipedia:arcade cabinet|cabinets]] were depicted. The trench sequence was seen in ''Gremlins'' when the Gremlins play the game and in the film ''No Small Affair'', an upright cabinet is used.
